data_safety_mindset.label: Data Safety Mindset
data_safety_mindset.summary: Learn why previewing matters, why SELECT-first habits help, why backups and caution matter, and how to think before changing stored data.

sketch0.title: Why preview before update or delete?

sketch2.title: Why do backups and caution matter?

sketch3.title: What does it mean to think before changing data?

preview_before_update_delete.title: Previewing first helps you see the exact rows you are about to change.
preview_before_update_delete.bodyMarkdown:
A strong beginner habit is:

- preview first
- modify second

That means using a SELECT query with the same WHERE condition before you run an UPDATE or DELETE.

So the big idea is:

> previewing first helps confirm the target rows before changing them

using_select_first.title: Using SELECT first is one of the safest habits in beginner SQL.
using_select_first.bodyMarkdown:
SELECT does not change the data.

That makes it a safe way to ask:

- which rows match?
- is this really the target set?
- did my condition catch what I expected?

So the key idea is:

> SELECT-first thinking is a safe way to check your logic before changing data

backups_and_caution.title: Backups and caution matter because change queries can affect real stored data.
backups_and_caution.bodyMarkdown:
When a query changes or removes data, mistakes can matter more.

That is why good habits include:

- caution before running the statement
- checking the target carefully
- understanding that data changes are more serious than read-only queries

So the main idea is:

> change queries deserve more caution than simple SELECT queries

thinking_before_changing_data.title: Thinking before changing data means asking what will change and why.
thinking_before_changing_data.bodyMarkdown:
Before running an update or delete, a beginner should ask:

- what rows will this touch?
- is the WHERE condition correct?
- should I preview first?
- am I sure this is the intended change?

So the important idea is:

> safe data changes begin with careful thinking, not rushing

m12_s03_preview_status_rows.title: Preview before changing
m12_s03_preview_status_rows.prompt:
The SQLite workspace already has the `inventory_ops` dataset loaded.

Write a query that returns all columns from `inventory_items` where `status` = 'draft'.

Then click **Run** and inspect which rows would be affected by a future change.

help.concept: A preview query helps confirm the exact target rows before running an UPDATE or DELETE.
help.hint_1: Use a SELECT query with the same condition you might later use in a change query.
help.hint_2: Build a preview query that shows the full rows matching the draft status so you can confirm the target set first.

m12_s03_preview_status_rows.hint: Use SELECT first with the same WHERE condition.
m12_s03_preview_status_rows.starterCode: -- Preview the rows that match status = 'draft'

m12_s03_preview_test_rows.title: Use SELECT first for test rows
m12_s03_preview_test_rows.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns all columns from `inventory_items` where `is_test` = 1.

Then run it and inspect which rows would be affected by a future delete.

help.concept: Using SELECT first is a safe way to confirm that your targeting condition matches the intended rows.
help.hint_1: Use a normal SELECT query with the same condition you might later use in DELETE.
help.hint_2: Build a preview query that shows the rows marked as test rows so you can verify the target set before removing anything.

m12_s03_preview_test_rows.hint: Use SELECT first with the future delete condition.
m12_s03_preview_test_rows.starterCode: -- Preview the rows marked as test rows

m12_s03_preview_single_row.title: Preview one row before updating
m12_s03_preview_single_row.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns all columns from `inventory_items` where `id` = 4.

Then click **Run** and inspect the row that would be changed by a future update.

help.concept: Previewing one specific row can make later updates much safer and easier to reason about.
help.hint_1: Use SELECT with a WHERE condition on the item id.
help.hint_2: Build a full-row preview query for the single target row so you can check exactly what would be changed.

m12_s03_preview_single_row.hint: Use SELECT * with a WHERE condition on the row id.
m12_s03_preview_single_row.starterCode: -- Preview one row before a future update

m12_s03_compare_preview_and_change_minds.title: Practice the preview mindset
m12_s03_compare_preview_and_change_minds.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns all columns from `inventory_items` where `price` > 10.

Then run it and think about why previewing this target set matters before a future update.

help.concept: Previewing broad target sets helps learners notice how many rows a future change might affect.
help.hint_1: Use a SELECT query with a WHERE condition on price.
help.hint_2: Build a preview query that shows all rows above the price threshold so you can see the size of the future target set.

m12_s03_compare_preview_and_change_minds.hint: Use SELECT first to see the full target set.
m12_s03_compare_preview_and_change_minds.starterCode: -- Preview rows where price is greater than 10

m12_s03_safety_mindset_project.title: Practice the data safety mindset
m12_s03_safety_mindset_project.prompt:
Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice the safety mindset for data changes on the `inventory_ops` dataset.

For each task:

1. write a SELECT preview query first
2. click **Run**
3. inspect which rows match
4. think about what an update or delete would affect

Tasks:

1. Preview rows where `status` = 'draft'
2. Preview rows where `is_test` = 1
3. Preview one row by id before a future update
4. Preview rows where `price` > 10
5. Preview rows where `category` = 'Decor'

Run one query at a time.
This project is about building safe thinking before writing change queries.

help.concept: The safety mindset grows when learners repeatedly preview target rows before imagining changes to them.
help.hint_1: For each task, use SELECT with the same condition you would later use in a change query.
help.hint_2: Solve the tasks one by one. Focus on whether the previewed target set matches what you would actually want to change.

m12_s03_safety_mindset_project.hint: Use SELECT first and study the target rows carefully.

m12_s03_preview_meaning.title: Why preview before update or delete?
m12_s03_preview_meaning.prompt: Why is previewing rows before an update or delete such a good beginner habit?

help.concept: Previewing helps confirm the exact rows that would be affected before making changes.
help.hint_2: It is a safe way to check whether your WHERE condition really matches what you intended.

m12_s03_preview_meaning.hint: Think confirm the exact target rows first.

options.a: It helps confirm the exact rows that would be affected
options.c: It removes the need for WHERE

m12_s03_select_first_meaning.title: Why use SELECT first?
m12_s03_select_first_meaning.prompt: Why is SELECT-first thinking so useful before data changes?

help.concept: SELECT lets you inspect the target rows without changing them.
help.hint_2: It is useful because it checks the logic without modifying the data.

m12_s03_select_first_meaning.hint: Think inspect safely before changing anything.

options.a: Because SELECT lets you inspect the target rows without changing them

m12_s03_backups_meaning.title: Why do backups and caution matter?
m12_s03_backups_meaning.prompt: Why do backups and caution matter more with update and delete queries than with simple SELECT queries?

help.concept: Update and delete queries modify stored data, so mistakes can matter more.
help.hint_1: Think real changes to stored rows.
help.hint_2: The risk is higher because the query is not just reading data anymore.

m12_s03_backups_meaning.hint: Think change queries affect real stored data.

options.a: Because update and delete queries change stored data
options.b: Because SELECT is more dangerous than DELETE
options.c: Because backups only matter for comments

m12_s03_thinking_before_change_meaning.title: What does it mean to think before changing data?
m12_s03_thinking_before_change_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of thinking before changing stored data?

help.concept: It means checking what rows will change and whether the condition is truly correct before running the change.
help.hint_1: Think what rows, why those rows, and is the condition right?
help.hint_2: It is a pause for reasoning before action.

m12_s03_thinking_before_change_meaning.hint: Think which rows will change and whether that is really correct.

options.a: Check what rows will change and whether the condition is really correct
options.b: Run the change first and inspect later only

m12_s03_change_mindset_goal.title: What is the goal of a data safety mindset?
m12_s03_change_mindset_goal.prompt: What is the main goal of a data safety mindset in beginner SQL?

help.concept: The safety mindset aims to reduce preventable mistakes before change queries are run.
help.hint_2: The goal is to build habits that make data changes safer and more deliberate.

m12_s03_change_mindset_goal.hint: Think safer, more deliberate data changes.

options.a: Reduce preventable mistakes before running change queries
options.b: Make update and delete queries more rushed

m12_s03_preview_true.title: What is true about previewing before data changes?
m12_s03_preview_true.prompt: Select all true statements about previewing before updates or deletes.

help.concept: Previewing is a safety habit that helps confirm the exact target rows before modification.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about using read-only checks before write queries.

options.a: Previewing can confirm the target rows
options.b: Previewing often uses a SELECT query
options.c: Previewing can reveal if the target set is larger than expected
options.e: Previewing is a strong beginner safety habit

m12_s03_select_first_true.title: What is true about using SELECT first?
m12_s03_select_first_true.prompt: Select all true statements about using SELECT first before updates or deletes.

help.concept: SELECT-first thinking helps learners test conditions safely before modifying rows.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about inspecting rows without changing them.

options.a: SELECT can preview the rows matched by a future change condition
options.b: SELECT can help verify WHERE logic safely
options.c: SELECT does not modify the data while previewing
options.d: SELECT always performs the update too
options.e: SELECT-first thinking can reduce change mistakes

m12_s03_backups_true.title: What is true about backups and caution?
m12_s03_backups_true.prompt: Select all true statements about backups and caution around data changes.

help.concept: Backups and caution matter because change queries are more serious than read-only queries.
help.hint_1: Think stored data can be changed or removed.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about treating write queries with more care.

m12_s03_backups_true.hint: Think change queries deserve more caution.

options.a: Change queries deserve more caution than simple reads
options.b: Mistakes in updates or deletes can matter more
options.c: Careful habits are part of safer data work
options.d: Backups and caution are useless with data changes
options.e: Thinking ahead is part of safe modification work

m12_s03_safety_mindset_workflow_true.title: What matches a good beginner workflow for a data safety mindset?
m12_s03_safety_mindset_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good beginner workflow for safe data changes.

help.concept: A good workflow is to identify the target rows, preview them, confirm the WHERE logic, and only then consider running the change.
help.hint_1: Think identify, preview, confirm, then change.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that build safe habits before touching stored data.

m12_s03_safety_mindset_workflow_true.hint: Identify the rows first, preview them, then confirm the change logic.

options.a: Decide what rows should change or be removed
options.b: Preview those rows with SELECT first
options.c: Confirm that the WHERE condition matches the intended target set
options.d: Run the change only after that check
options.e: Skip previewing because it slows you down
